title: GitHub Copilot
passing_note: GH-300 passes at 700/1000. Microsoft does not publish the raw-score mapping, so the readiness estimate below is unofficial and based on percent correct only.

readiness_bands.label: Ready
readiness_bands.note: Consistently strong — you're tracking well above the pass line.

readiness_bands.label: Approaching
readiness_bands.note: Close — shore up weaker domains before test day.

readiness_bands.label: Not ready yet
readiness_bands.note: Keep studying — focus on your lowest-scoring domains.

domains.title: Understand GitHub Copilot data and architecture
Apply prompt engineering and context crafting
Improve developer productivity with GitHub Copilot
Configure privacy, content exclusions, and safeguards

questions.stem: While pairing with GitHub Copilot, a developer accepts a suggestion that calls a helper method on a popular library. The build fails because that method does not exist in any version of the library. Which limitation of generative AI does this best illustrate?

options.text: Bias
Hallucination
Data minimization
Envelope encryption

questions.explanation: Hallucination is when the model produces confident, plausible-looking output that references APIs or facts that do not actually exist. Bias is skewed patterns learned from training data, not fabricated references. Data minimization and envelope encryption are privacy/security mitigations, not model limitations.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot responsibly

questions.stem: A team notices Copilot keeps recommending an older API pattern even though a newer, better-supported API has shipped. What is the most accurate explanation?

options.text: The model's knowledge is limited to its training data up to a cutoff, so it may not know newer frameworks or changes.
The model genuinely understands the code and prefers the older pattern for safety.
Copilot never suggests library calls, only comments.
Newer APIs are automatically blocked by responsible-AI policy.

questions.explanation: Generative models only 'know' what was in their training data up to a cutoff and do not automatically learn about changes made afterward, so they can propose stale patterns. Copilot does not have genuine understanding, does suggest code, and there is no policy that blocks newer APIs.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot responsibly

questions.stem: Which of the following are recognized limitations of generative AI tools like GitHub Copilot? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: It can hallucinate output that is plausible but incorrect.
It can reflect and amplify bias present in its training data.
Its knowledge can be stale relative to its training cutoff.
It guarantees generated code is secure and correct.

questions.explanation: Hallucination, bias, and stale knowledge are all documented limitations, and suggestions come from pattern prediction rather than true reasoning. Because of this, output is never guaranteed to be secure or correct, so option D is false.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot responsibly

questions.stem: How many principles make up Microsoft and GitHub's framework for responsible AI?

options.text: Four
Five
Six
Eight

questions.explanation: There are exactly six principles: fairness, reliability and safety, privacy and security, inclusiveness, transparency, and accountability. Distractors that add items such as 'explainability' or 'efficiency' as separate official principles are incorrect.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot responsibly

questions.stem: A team wants Copilot-related tooling to work well for developers who use screen readers and who work in several different languages. Which responsible-AI principle does this goal most directly support?

options.text: Reliability and safety
Inclusiveness
Accountability
Privacy and security

questions.explanation: Inclusiveness means AI systems should empower everyone and be accessible across abilities, languages, and geographies. Reliability and safety concerns predictable behavior, accountability concerns human responsibility, and privacy and security concerns protecting data.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot responsibly

questions.stem: Which responsible-AI principle is most directly about making an AI system understandable and being honest about its capabilities and limitations?

options.text: Transparency
Fairness
Inclusiveness
Reliability and safety

questions.explanation: Transparency requires that AI systems be interpretable and that creators be honest about capabilities and limitations. Fairness targets equal treatment, inclusiveness targets accessibility, and reliability and safety targets consistent, safe behavior.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot responsibly

questions.stem: True or false: Because the AI generated the code, no person is responsible for the outcome.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. The accountability principle keeps people — not the software — responsible for how AI systems operate and for the code they accept. Responsibility is never transferred to the tool.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot responsibly

questions.stem: A model consistently gives lower scores to one demographic group for otherwise similar inputs. Which set of mitigations most directly targets this harm?

options.text: Fairness techniques such as reviewing training data, testing with balanced samples, adversarial debiasing, and controls to override unfair scores
Envelope encryption and hardware security modules
Rotating encryption keys and running security audits
Obtaining user consent and minimizing data collection

questions.explanation: Unfair differential impact is a fairness harm, addressed with fairness techniques like reviewing training data, testing with balanced demographic samples, adversarial debiasing, and override controls. The other options are privacy and security mitigations that do not address bias.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot responsibly

questions.stem: Which privacy technique replaces personal details with random identifiers so that records cannot easily be tied to individuals?

options.text: Aggregation
Pseudonymization
Adversarial debiasing
Key rotation

questions.explanation: Pseudonymization swaps identifying fields for random identifiers. Aggregation summarizes data into groups to remove individual detail, adversarial debiasing is a fairness technique, and key rotation is a security control.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot responsibly

questions.stem: Which of the following are cross-cutting mitigations that apply across nearly every AI risk? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Robust governance frameworks
Transparency in AI processes
Human oversight
Accepting all suggestions to maximize speed

questions.explanation: Governance frameworks, transparency in processes, and human oversight are the three cross-cutting mitigations that reduce risk everywhere. Accepting all suggestions without review abandons oversight and increases risk, so option D is not a mitigation.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot responsibly

questions.stem: Why is human validation of Copilot output considered necessary rather than optional?

options.text: Generative AI can hallucinate, encode bias, and rely on stale knowledge, so output is not guaranteed correct or secure.
Copilot output is always correct, so validation is only a formality.
Validation transfers responsibility for the code from the developer to Copilot.
Validation is required only for comments, not for code.

questions.explanation: Because generative AI can hallucinate, be biased, and use outdated knowledge, a human must review and test suggestions before trusting them. Output is not always correct, validation does not offload responsibility to the tool, and it applies to code as well as comments.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot responsibly

questions.stem: A developer accepts a Copilot suggestion that later causes a production defect. Under the responsible-AI principles, who is accountable for the defect?

options.text: GitHub Copilot, because it generated the code
No one, because AI-generated code has no owner
The developer who reviewed and accepted the suggestion
The training-data provider

questions.explanation: Accountability stays with people; once a developer accepts a suggestion, they own it. Copilot assists but does not assume responsibility for correctness, security, or licensing of committed code.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot responsibly

questions.stem: Which sequence best describes the validate-output loop a developer should apply to a Copilot suggestion?

options.text: Accept the suggestion, then investigate only if a bug is reported later.
Read the suggestion, check correctness, security, licensing, and fit, test it, then accept, edit, or reject.
Reject every suggestion and write all code manually.
Forward the suggestion to Copilot for automatic self-approval.

questions.explanation: The validation loop is: read the suggestion, check correctness/security/licensing/fit, test it, then decide to accept, edit, or reject — with a human in the loop. Accepting blindly, rejecting everything, or expecting the tool to self-approve are all incorrect.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot responsibly

questions.stem: To move faster, a developer configures their workflow to accept every Copilot suggestion without reviewing it. Which responsible-use practice is being violated?

options.text: Data minimization
Human oversight and validation of output
Key rotation
Aggregation

questions.explanation: Responsible operation requires reviewing and validating suggestions; skipping review abandons human oversight and accountability. Data minimization, key rotation, and aggregation are privacy/security mitigations unrelated to reviewing suggestions.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot responsibly

questions.stem: Which practices describe operating GitHub Copilot responsibly within an organization? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Keep a human reviewing and validating output
Ensure generated code aligns with ethical standards and project-specific requirements
Use governance frameworks and continuously monitor performance
Disable code review so suggestions can be accepted faster

questions.explanation: Responsible operation combines productivity with human oversight, alignment to standards and requirements, governance, and ongoing monitoring. Disabling review to accept suggestions faster abandons oversight and is explicitly not responsible use.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot responsibly

questions.stem: True or false: Gaining productivity from Copilot and operating it responsibly are opposing goals, so teams must sacrifice oversight to move faster.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. The goal is to use Copilot effectively while mitigating ethical and operational risks — the two goals go together. Productivity and responsibility are pursued together, not traded off, and oversight remains part of responsible operation.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot responsibly

questions.stem: A developer wants to use GitHub Copilot to explain code directly on GitHub.com, but a teammate insists they first install a browser extension. What is the correct guidance?

options.text: Install the GitHub Copilot browser extension before Copilot will work on GitHub.com
No extension is needed — Copilot is built into GitHub.com; extensions are only required for IDEs
Copilot is unavailable on GitHub.com and only runs in VS Code and JetBrains IDEs
They must switch to Neovim because GitHub.com does not support Copilot chat

questions.explanation: GitHub.com requires no extension — Copilot is built into the web interface. Extensions are only needed in IDEs such as VS Code, Visual Studio, JetBrains, and Neovim. Copilot is fully available on GitHub.com, so options a, c, and d are incorrect.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: In which environments does GitHub Copilot run as a supported surface? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: GitHub.com
Visual Studio Code
JetBrains IDEs
Neovim
Microsoft Word

questions.explanation: Copilot is supported on GitHub.com (no extension needed), VS Code, Visual Studio, JetBrains IDEs, and Neovim. Microsoft Word is not a supported Copilot coding surface, so option e is incorrect.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: A developer highlights a single function and wants a quick, in-place explanation without switching to the full Chat panel or leaving the editor cursor. Which trigger best fits?

options.text: Inline chat (Ctrl+I) with the /explain slash command
Accepting the inline ghost-text suggestion with Tab
Agent Mode running a terminal command
The Copilot CLI in a separate terminal window

questions.explanation: Inline chat opens at the cursor with Ctrl+I (Cmd+I on macOS) and supports slash commands like /explain, giving focused, context-specific help without opening the main Chat panel. Ghost text autocompletes but does not explain; Agent Mode and the CLI are heavier, less focused tools for this task.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: A task requires touching several files, installing a dependency, and running the test suite until it passes — all driven by Copilot with minimal manual steps. Which interaction mode is designed for this?

options.text: Inline suggestions (ghost text)
The Copilot Chat panel answering a single question
Agent Mode
Comments-to-code generation

questions.explanation: Agent Mode works autonomously and iteratively: it determines relevant files and dependencies, runs terminal commands such as installs and tests, and self-heals until the task succeeds. Inline suggestions, a single chat answer, and comments-to-code do not run terminal commands or iterate across multiple steps.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: A security team on Copilot Business excludes the secrets/ folder via content exclusions, yet notices Chat still references a symbol defined in that folder. What best explains this behavior?

options.text: Content exclusions are disabled whenever Chat is used
Semantic information (types/hover definitions) can still surface from non-excluded files, and the @github chat participant does not honor exclusions
Exclusions only take effect after the repository is archived
The team must upgrade to Enterprise for exclusions to work in Chat

questions.explanation: Content exclusions block completions and context from the excluded file itself, but documented IDE limitations mean semantic info (type/hover definitions) referenced from a non-excluded file can still appear, and exclusions are not applied to the @github chat participant. Exclusions are not disabled by Chat, do not depend on archiving, and are available on both Business and Enterprise.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: Content exclusions for specific files and repositories are available on the Copilot Pro plan.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. Content exclusions are a management capability available only on Copilot Business and Copilot Enterprise. Free and Pro do not offer content exclusions.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: A developer asks whether GitHub Copilot CLI is the same program as the GitHub CLI (gh). What is the accurate description of their relationship?

options.text: They are the same binary; copilot is just an alias for gh
Copilot CLI replaces gh and gh is deprecated
Copilot CLI runs independently from gh but reuses your existing GitHub authentication, and the two can be combined
Copilot CLI requires a separate, non-GitHub account to sign in

questions.explanation: Copilot CLI brings Copilot to the terminal and runs independently from the GitHub CLI, but it authenticates with your existing GitHub credentials and complements gh for repo and issue tasks. It is not the same binary, does not deprecate gh, and does not use a separate non-GitHub account.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: A developer wants a single explanation of a shell command from Copilot CLI without entering a full interactive session. Which command should they run?

options.text: copilot -i "explain brew install git"
copilot (with no arguments)
gh copilot session start
copilot --cloud

questions.explanation: The -i one-shot flag returns a single answer without launching interactive mode, e.g. copilot -i "explain brew install git". Running copilot with no arguments starts interactive mode; copilot --cloud opens a cloud-sandboxed session; gh copilot session start is not the documented one-shot pattern.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: In an interactive Copilot CLI session, a developer types "please switch to a different model" as plain text, but the model does not change. Why?

options.text: Model switching requires the /model slash command; natural language cannot change session settings
Model switching is only possible by restarting the terminal
The Free plan forbids changing models entirely
Natural language changed the model, but the confirmation message was suppressed

questions.explanation: Slash commands are the only way to control session settings and configuration; they cannot be replaced by natural language. Changing the model requires the /model slash command. Natural language handles explain, suggest, and revise — not configuration.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: Which pairing correctly matches a Copilot CLI slash command to its purpose?

options.text: /mcp manages MCP servers, and /skills manages enhanced capabilities
/model changes the color theme, and /theme selects the AI model
/exit installs new tools, and /help exits the session
/suggest can only be triggered by the /model command

questions.explanation: /mcp manages MCP servers and /skills manages enhanced capabilities. /model selects the AI model and /theme changes the theme (not the reverse); /exit ends the session and /help shows help; /suggest is a natural-language-capable request, unrelated to /model.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: A developer wants to begin work in a Copilot CLI session on one laptop and resume it later on a different machine without copying files around. Which option supports this?

options.text: Local sandboxing with /sandbox enable
Cloud sandboxing with copilot --cloud
One-shot mode with copilot -i
Attaching a file with @ in interactive mode

questions.explanation: Cloud sandboxing (copilot --cloud) runs in a fully isolated GitHub-hosted Linux environment; a stopped session snapshots its state so it can be resumed across devices. Local sandboxing uses the current machine's resources, one-shot mode returns a single answer, and @ only attaches file context.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: Members of an organization report they cannot start a cloud-sandboxed Copilot CLI session. What is the most likely governance cause?

options.text: They forgot to run brew install copilot-cli
The org/enterprise Cloud Sandbox access policy has not been enabled by an admin
Cloud sessions require the Free plan to be downgraded first
They must delete all Stopped sessions before an Active one can start

questions.explanation: Organization/enterprise admins must enable the Cloud Sandbox policy before members can use cloud sessions. Installation with Homebrew relates to setup, cloud sessions are not tied to downgrading Free, and cloud session states (Active/Stopped/Deleted) do not block starting a new session.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: Copilot CLI access to your machine is governed along which permission axes? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Trusted directories
Tool permissions (--allow-tool / --deny-tool)
Path permissions
URL permissions
Screen-brightness permissions

questions.explanation: Copilot CLI permissions are scoped by trusted directories, tool permissions (--allow-tool / --deny-tool), path permissions, and URL permissions. Screen brightness is not a permission axis, so option e is incorrect.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: A developer assigns a GitHub issue to Copilot, then adds clarifying requirements as a new comment on that issue, but the Cloud Agent ignores them. What is the correct way to steer the agent after it starts?

options.text: Delete and re-create the issue so the agent re-reads it
Mention @copilot in a comment on the agent's pull request (write permission required)
Restart the GitHub Actions runner from the org settings
Edit the copilot/ branch name to include the new requirements

questions.explanation: The Cloud Agent receives the issue content at assignment time; later comments on the issue are not seen. To guide it after it starts, mention @copilot on the agent's pull request (which requires write permission). Re-creating the issue, restarting runners, or renaming the branch are not the documented mechanisms.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: A team wants Copilot's agent to reach external tools and enterprise data through a standardized, vendor-neutral interface. The GitHub-hosted server for this is added over HTTP and authorized with OAuth. Which technology is being described?

options.text: The Model Context Protocol (MCP), via the GitHub MCP Server at https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/
A branch ruleset requiring pull request review
Content exclusions configured at the organization level
The GitHub CLI gh issue edit command

questions.explanation: MCP is an open standard ("USB-C for AI tools") that connects models to external tools and data. The hosted GitHub MCP Server is at https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/ and is added via MCP: Add server > HTTP, then authorized with OAuth. Rulesets, content exclusions, and gh issue edit serve unrelated purposes.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: A Copilot code review on GitHub.com counts toward a branch's required approvals for merging.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. Copilot's review is advisory: it does not approve or reject a pull request and its comments do not count toward required approvals. A human reviewer must still approve before merge.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: An administrator must ensure Copilot automatically reviews every pull request targeting main in a production repository — not just one user's PRs. What should they configure?

options.text: Each developer's account-level Automatic Copilot code review toggle
A branch ruleset that requires a pull request before merging and requests pull request review from Copilot
A content exclusion on the main branch
A copilot-instructions.md file with an applyTo glob

questions.explanation: Rulesets enforce automatic Copilot review at the repo/org level: create a branch ruleset that requires a pull request before merging and requests pull request review from Copilot. The personal Automatic Copilot code review toggle (Pro/Pro+) only covers one user's PRs; content exclusions and instructions files do not enforce reviews.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: A manager worries that sharing a Copilot Space could expose private repositories to teammates who lack access to them. Is that a valid concern?

options.text: Yes — sharing a Space grants all viewers read access to every attached repository
No — Spaces inherit GitHub permissions and grant no new access; viewers only see content they are already entitled to
Yes — Spaces bypass permissions because they reference the main branch
No — because Spaces can only ever contain free-text notes, never repositories

questions.explanation: Copilot Spaces inherit GitHub permissions and grant no new access, so only users already entitled to the linked content see it reflected in answers. Sharing a Space does not grant repo read access, does not bypass permissions, and Spaces can attach files, issues, PRs, and uploads — not only text.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: While debugging a complex multi-threaded issue, a developer selects a premium reasoning model in Copilot Chat instead of a standard model. What is the main trade-off to keep in mind?

options.text: Premium reasoning models roughly double PRU consumption (~2 vs ~1) compared with standard models
Premium models disable the ability to reference files with #file:
Premium models remove the thumbs up/down feedback controls
Premium models are only available on the Free plan

questions.explanation: Standard models (e.g., GPT-4o) cost about 1 PRU per request while premium reasoning models (e.g., o1-preview/o1-mini) cost about 2 PRUs — roughly double — so they should be reserved for genuinely complex tasks. Model choice does not disable #file: scoping or feedback controls, and premium models are not Free-only.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: A developer wants to focus a Copilot Chat question on the contents of a single file named controller.js. Which reference should they use?

options.text: @terminal controller.js
#file:controller.js
/optimize controller.js
@vscode controller.js

questions.explanation: The #file: scope tells Chat to focus on a specific file's contents, e.g. #file:controller.js. @terminal and @vscode are participants that scope to a tool/context, not a file, and /optimize is a slash command that refactors code rather than scoping the question to a file.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: An enterprise legal team requires IP indemnity coverage for all Copilot suggestions across the organization. Which setting is mandatory to qualify?

options.text: Set Suggestions matching public code to Block for the organization
Set Suggestions matching public code to Allow for the organization
Enable content exclusions on every repository
Require GitHub Enterprise Cloud for all members

questions.explanation: IP indemnity applies only when the Matching public code setting is set to Block; allowing matching suggestions forfeits indemnity coverage. Content exclusions and requiring Enterprise Cloud are separate features and are not the precondition for IP indemnity.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: Which management and security features are available on Copilot Business or Enterprise but not on Free or Pro? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Content exclusions
Usage metrics
IP indemnity
The public code (matching) filter

questions.explanation: Content exclusions, usage metrics, and IP indemnity are Business/Enterprise management and security features. The public code filter is available on all plans, so option d is not exclusive to Business/Enterprise.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: A compliance officer needs an auditable record of every change an autonomous agent made to a repository. Which Copilot capability provides this traceability?

options.text: The Cloud Agent's session logs and pull request history
Local IDE Agent Mode sessions, which are logged to GitHub automatically
The inline suggestion cache in VS Code
The @github chat participant's response history

questions.explanation: All Cloud Agent work is committed to GitHub with visible session logs and pull request timelines, providing durable traceability of every commit and action — an advantage over private local sessions. Local IDE agent sessions are not automatically committed to GitHub, and inline caches or chat responses are not audit trails.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: A platform team wants to delegate issues to Copilot from an automated pipeline instead of clicking through GitHub.com. Which approach aligns with the documented programmatic pattern?

options.text: Use the GitHub GraphQL API (suggestedActors with CAN_BE_ASSIGNED, then replaceActorsForAssignable) or the gh CLI (gh issue edit)
Edit the copilot-instructions.md file to auto-assign issues
Enable a content exclusion so Copilot picks up issues automatically
Add Copilot to the Reviewers menu on each pull request

questions.explanation: Copilot workflows can be driven programmatically: check availability with GraphQL suggestedActors (capability CAN_BE_ASSIGNED), then use createIssue or replaceActorsForAssignable, or run gh issue edit from the CLI. Instructions files, content exclusions, and the Reviewers menu serve different purposes and do not automate issue delegation.
questions.domain_title: Use GitHub Copilot features

questions.stem: A developer accepts an inline code completion in the editor and worries that the prompt used to generate it is being stored to retrain the foundational models. For a standard editor completion, what actually happens to that prompt?

options.text: It is retained for 28 days and then deleted.
It is discarded once the suggestion is returned and is not used to train the foundational models.
It is stored permanently on the developer's machine for auditing.
It is always used to fine-tune the model unless the organization opts out.

questions.explanation: Copilot in the code editor does not retain completion prompts; they are discarded once the suggestion is returned and are not used to train the foundational models. The 28-day retention applies to Chat/CLI/Mobile outside the editor, not inline completions.
questions.domain_title: Understand GitHub Copilot data and architecture

questions.stem: Which GitHub Copilot plan lets a user opt out of having their prompts used to fine-tune GitHub's foundational model?

options.text: Copilot Business
Copilot Enterprise
Copilot Individual
No plan allows opting out

questions.explanation: Individual subscribers can opt out of sharing prompts that would otherwise be used to fine-tune GitHub's foundational model. Business and Enterprise data is not used for training in the first place, so no opt-out is needed there.
questions.domain_title: Understand GitHub Copilot data and architecture

questions.stem: Which statements about GitHub Copilot data handling and flow are accurate? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: The prompt is transmitted to Copilot's servers over HTTPS.
A proxy service hosted in a GitHub-owned Microsoft Azure tenant sits between the client and the model.
Prompts and suggestions on Business and Enterprise plans are used to train the models.
For Copilot Chat used outside the editor, GitHub typically retains prompts, suggestions, and supporting context for about 28 days.

questions.explanation: Prompts travel over HTTPS to Copilot's servers, and a proxy in a GitHub-owned Azure tenant filters traffic between client and model. Chat/CLI/Mobile outside the editor is typically retained ~28 days. Option C is false: organizational (Business/Enterprise) prompts and suggestions are not used to train the models.
questions.domain_title: Understand GitHub Copilot data and architecture

questions.stem: A developer types a short comment describing a function they want. When Copilot builds the prompt it sends to the model, which best describes what that prompt contains?

options.text: Only the literal comment text the developer typed.
The entire repository, uploaded in full to the model.
An assembled prompt combining the developer's text with gathered editor context such as surrounding code, filename/type, open tabs, and project structure.
Only the code located above the cursor position.

questions.explanation: The prompt Copilot sends is richer than what you typed. It assembles the request together with context like code before and after the cursor, filename and file type, adjacent open tabs, project structure/file paths, and the languages/frameworks in use.
questions.domain_title: Understand GitHub Copilot data and architecture

questions.stem: Copilot uses a pre-processing technique that considers both the code preceding the cursor and the code following it to produce more relevant suggestions. What is this technique called?

options.text: Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM)
Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)
Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF)
Token truncation

questions.explanation: Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM) considers both the preceding and the following code, widening the model's view for more accurate, relevant suggestions. A common distractor is that Copilot only uses code above the cursor.
questions.domain_title: Understand GitHub Copilot data and architecture

questions.stem: A security reviewer asks what defends Copilot against users trying to manipulate the system into revealing how it generates code (prompt injection). Which component provides this inbound defense?

options.text: The optional matching-public-code filter
The outbound code-quality check
The proxy service in the GitHub-owned Azure tenant, which filters traffic and blocks attempts to hack the prompt
The editor's local autocomplete cache

questions.explanation: On the inbound path the proxy (hosted in a GitHub-owned Microsoft Azure tenant) filters traffic and blocks attempts to hack the prompt or coax the system into revealing how it generates suggestions. The public-code and code-quality filters run on the outbound path.
questions.domain_title: Understand GitHub Copilot data and architecture

questions.stem: During outbound post-processing, which checks does Copilot apply to the model's generated response before delivering it? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: A toxicity filter that removes harmful generated content.
A code-quality scan for vulnerabilities such as cross-site scripting (XSS) and SQL injection.
An optional, administrator-enabled filter that blocks suggestions over ~150 characters that closely resemble existing public code.
A mandatory 28-day retention of the suggestion for all plans.

questions.explanation: On the outbound path the toxicity filter removes harmful content, the proxy runs a code-quality check for patterns like XSS and SQL injection, and an optional admin-enabled public-code filter blocks longer (~150+ character) look-alikes. Content that fails is truncated or discarded. Retention is not part of outbound validation and is not mandatory across all plans.
questions.domain_title: Understand GitHub Copilot data and architecture

questions.stem: True or False: The matching-public-code filter is always on for every GitHub Copilot user and cannot be changed by an administrator.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. The matching-public-code (duplication-detection) filter is optional and administrator-enabled. It targets suggestions over ~150 characters that closely resemble existing public code, and it is not always on.
questions.domain_title: Understand GitHub Copilot data and architecture

questions.stem: Place the following stages of the code-suggestion lifecycle in the correct order: generate, post-process, capture context, deliver.

options.text: Generate → capture context → deliver → post-process
Capture context → generate → post-process → deliver
Capture context → post-process → generate → deliver
Deliver → capture context → generate → post-process

questions.explanation: Context is assembled first, the LLM generates candidate suggestions, outbound filters validate/post-process the response, and only surviving content is delivered. Delivery is gated on passing all filters.
questions.domain_title: Understand GitHub Copilot data and architecture

questions.stem: When a developer accepts, modifies, or rejects a Copilot suggestion, which part of the suggestion lifecycle does that action contribute to?

options.text: The inbound toxicity filter
The feedback loop that refines Copilot's intent understanding over time
The HTTPS transmission step
The retraining of the foundational model on the developer's code

questions.explanation: Accept/modify/reject actions feed Copilot's feedback loop, which refines its understanding of intent for future prompts. It is about improving intent understanding, not retaining the developer's code for model training.
questions.domain_title: Understand GitHub Copilot data and architecture

questions.stem: Copilot suggests a function that calls a library method that does not actually exist. Which LLM limitation does this illustrate, and what is the recommended response?

options.text: A context-window limit; open more tabs so the model can see everything.
Hallucination from probabilistic generation; validate the output against the API/docs and test it.
A retention-policy limit; disable prompt sharing.
A proxy-filtering failure; re-enable the public-code filter.

questions.explanation: LLM output is probabilistic — the model predicts plausible code that may be incorrect or reference non-existent APIs (hallucination). The mitigation is to validate the output and keep a human in the loop rather than trusting confident-looking suggestions.
questions.domain_title: Understand GitHub Copilot data and architecture

questions.stem: A large refactor spanning many files produces suggestions that ignore logic in files the developer has not opened. Which LLM limitation best explains this behavior?

options.text: The context window — the model can only see a bounded amount of code/tokens, so content outside it (including unopened files) is invisible.
IP indemnity restrictions on organizational plans.
The 28-day retention policy expiring mid-refactor.
The proxy discarding valid suggestions as toxic content.

questions.explanation: The context window is finite (standard completions ~200–500 lines / a few thousand tokens; Chat ~4k tokens). Anything outside that window, including files that aren't open, is invisible to the model, so large or cross-file problems may get incomplete answers. Breaking the task into smaller, focused prompts helps.
questions.domain_title: Understand GitHub Copilot data and architecture

questions.stem: A developer types the vague comment "// do the thing" above an empty function and receives unhelpful Copilot suggestions. According to prompt engineering guidance, which single change most improves the prompt's structure?

options.text: Rename the file so it ends in .test.js
State a clear, specific goal that names the inputs, expected types, and edge cases
Delete the comment and let Copilot infer everything from the function name
Paste the entire project README into the comment

questions.explanation: An effective prompt is built around a clear single goal plus supporting context. Making the goal explicit — inputs, types, and edge cases — is the core structural fix. A test-style filename (a) only nudges file type; removing the comment (c) reduces context; pasting an entire README (d) overloads the prompt and hurts clarity.
questions.domain_title: Apply prompt engineering and context crafting

questions.stem: The "prompt" that Copilot actually reasons over is only the literal comment or Chat text the developer types, with nothing added.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. The prompt Copilot reasons over is the assembled combination of your natural-language text plus the surrounding context it gathers (comments, related open files, code around the cursor). It is more than the literal characters you typed.
questions.domain_title: Apply prompt engineering and context crafting

questions.stem: A developer wants Copilot to factor in code that appears both before and after the cursor when generating a suggestion in the middle of a file. Which Copilot technique makes this possible?

options.text: Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM)
Zero-shot learning
Toxicity filtering
Role prompting

questions.explanation: Fill-in-the-Middle (FIM) is the pre-processing technique that considers both the preceding and following code, widening the model's view for more relevant suggestions. Zero-shot (b) is about examples; toxicity filtering (c) is a safety stage; role prompting (d) is a best practice — none of these describe using code after the cursor.
questions.domain_title: Apply prompt engineering and context crafting

questions.stem: Which of the following are context sources Copilot gathers from the editor session while processing a prompt? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Code before and after the cursor position
The filename and file type being edited
Adjacent open tabs in the editor
The developer's monitor resolution and mouse speed

questions.explanation: Copilot collects code before/after the cursor, the filename and file type, adjacent open tabs, project structure/file paths, and the languages and frameworks in use. Monitor resolution and mouse speed (d) are not part of the context Copilot gathers.
questions.domain_title: Apply prompt engineering and context crafting

questions.stem: A developer writes a one-line comment asking Copilot to generate a standard "reverse a string" utility and provides no examples. Which prompting approach is this?

options.text: Few-shot learning
One-shot learning
Zero-shot learning
Fine-tuning

questions.explanation: Zero-shot learning supplies no examples — Copilot relies solely on its foundational training, which works well for common patterns and standard functionality. One-shot (b) provides exactly one example, few-shot (a) provides several, and fine-tuning (d) retrains the model, which is not what a single comment does.
questions.domain_title: Apply prompt engineering and context crafting

questions.stem: A team needs Copilot to produce objects that exactly match an existing schema used across the repository, covering several edge cases. Which prompting approach is most appropriate?

options.text: Zero-shot — describe the schema in prose with no examples
Few-shot — provide several example objects that demonstrate the pattern
Toxicity filtering to enforce the schema
Disable all open tabs to reduce noise

questions.explanation: Few-shot prompting presents several examples, steering Copilot toward a precise, consistent pattern and helping it handle multiple scenarios and edge cases — ideal for matching an existing schema. Zero-shot (a) gives no pattern to follow; toxicity filtering (c) is a safety stage; closing tabs (d) removes helpful context.
questions.domain_title: Apply prompt engineering and context crafting

questions.stem: Copilot's first suggestion is close but misses one edge case. Following the recommended best practice, what should the developer do next?

options.text: Manually rewrite the suggestion from scratch without changing the prompt
Assert and iterate — erase the suggestion, enrich the comment with the missing detail or example, and prompt again
Accept the suggestion as-is and file a bug report
Switch to a different programming language

questions.explanation: The "assert and iterate" best practice says the first output may not be production-ready — rather than starting over by hand, erase it, add more detail or an example to the comment, and prompt again so each turn builds on Copilot's understanding. Manual rewriting (a) discards that iterative advantage; the others don't address the missing edge case.
questions.domain_title: Apply prompt engineering and context crafting

questions.stem: A team wants security-hardened code from Copilot on the very first attempt for a sensitive module. Which prompt-crafting best practice most directly helps?

options.text: Role prompting — instruct Copilot to "Act as a cybersecurity expert..."
Zero-shot prompting with no context
Summarizing the chat history
Enabling the matching-public-code filter

questions.explanation: Role prompting tells Copilot to "Act as a ..." (for example, a security expert) to get more targeted, domain-aware code on the first try. Zero-shot with no context (b) reduces specificity; summarizing chat history (c) is a performance lever; the public-code filter (d) is an outbound safety check, not a way to harden security logic.
questions.domain_title: Apply prompt engineering and context crafting

questions.stem: Within the 4 Ss of prompt engineering, which principle explains why keeping related files open in nearby tabs improves Copilot's suggestions?

options.text: Single
Specific
Short
Surround

questions.explanation: Surround means using descriptive filenames and keeping related files open so Copilot has rich context. Single (a) is about focusing on one task, Specific (b) about explicit detail, and Short (c) about conciseness — none of which describe the open-files/context behavior.
questions.domain_title: Apply prompt engineering and context crafting

questions.stem: Which principles make up the 4 Ss of prompt engineering described in the module? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Single
Specific
Short
Surround

questions.explanation: The 4 Ss are Single (focus on one task), Specific (explicit, detailed instructions), Short (concise prompts), and Surround (descriptive filenames and keeping related files open). All four options are correct.
questions.domain_title: Apply prompt engineering and context crafting

questions.stem: In Copilot's inbound prompt process flow, which sequence of stages is correct?

options.text: Secure transmission & context gathering, proxy filter, toxicity filtering, LLM generation
LLM generation, toxicity filtering, proxy filter, secure transmission & context gathering
Proxy filter, LLM generation, secure transmission & context gathering, toxicity filtering
Toxicity filtering, secure transmission & context gathering, LLM generation, proxy filter

questions.explanation: On the inbound path, the prompt is first sent over HTTPS while context is gathered (with FIM), then passes through the proxy filter (in a GitHub-owned Microsoft Azure tenant), then toxicity filtering, and finally reaches the LLM for generation. The other orderings misplace one or more stages.
questions.domain_title: Apply prompt engineering and context crafting

questions.stem: A Copilot Chat session has run for 20 turns and now feels sluggish and expensive, consuming an estimated 2–3 PRUs per turn. What is the recommended way to improve performance?

options.text: Keep appending the full history so Copilot never loses context
Summarize the relevant context or start a new conversation with focused context
Disable HTTPS transmission to speed up the request
Turn off Fill-in-the-Middle for the session

questions.explanation: Long prompts carrying the full conversation history can cost 2–3 PRUs per turn; summarizing context or resetting to a new conversation with focused context can keep it closer to 1 PRU per request and keeps context clean. Appending everything (a) makes it worse, and disabling HTTPS (c) or FIM (d) are not valid or recommended actions.
questions.domain_title: Apply prompt engineering and context crafting

questions.stem: A developer inherits a module that has no README and no docstrings. They want Copilot to draft a project README, add inline comments to complex functions, and describe each function's parameters and return values. Which Copilot productivity capability best matches this request?

options.text: Personalized code completion that adapts to coding style over time
Documentation writing, including inline comments, function descriptions, and README generation
Cross-language translation of the module's source code
Sample data generation for the test suite

questions.explanation: Copilot's documentation help spans inline comments explaining complex code, function descriptions (parameters and return values), and README structure/content drawn from the codebase. Personalized completion, cross-language translation, and sample data are different capabilities.
questions.domain_title: Improve developer productivity with GitHub Copilot

questions.stem: Under the module's "automating the boring stuff" theme, which tasks does GitHub Copilot help automate so developers can focus on complex, creative work? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Generating boilerplate code such as scaffolding a REST API or class structure
Writing unit tests
Refactoring and translating code between programming languages
Making final high-level software architecture decisions without human input

questions.explanation: "Automating the boring stuff" covers boilerplate generation, sample data, writing unit tests, and refactoring/cross-language translation. Copilot is strongest at code-level tasks; high-level architecture and novel design decisions should stay in human hands.
questions.domain_title: Improve developer productivity with GitHub Copilot

questions.stem: A developer new to Golang has Copilot generate a function but does not understand what it does. They want a plain-language walkthrough without leaving the IDE or opening a browser. What is the fastest path?

options.text: Run /setupTests to configure a test framework first
Use the "Explain this" context-menu option (or /explain) in the editor
Open external documentation in a web search to learn the syntax
Switch the file to a language Copilot already knows

questions.explanation: The "Explain this" option (or /explain in chat) provides an in-editor natural-language explanation of generated or unfamiliar code, accelerating learning while avoiding context switching. A web search would defeat the goal of staying in the editor.
questions.domain_title: Improve developer productivity with GitHub Copilot

questions.stem: A team lead wants to explain how Copilot reduces context switching for developers calling unfamiliar APIs. Which Copilot behavior most directly addresses that goal?

options.text: Providing quick references to correct method calls and parameters inline in the editor
Enforcing organization content exclusion policies
Generating performance benchmarks and load-testing scenarios
Configuring a test framework with /setupTests

questions.explanation: Reducing context switching comes from in-editor assistance, quick references (correct method calls and parameters for APIs and libraries), and code completion — the common thread is staying in the editor. Content exclusions are governance, not a focus/productivity benefit.
questions.domain_title: Improve developer productivity with GitHub Copilot

questions.stem: A tester needs 50 realistic-looking user records to exercise a registration form. What is the appropriate Copilot use, and what caveat applies?

options.text: Use Copilot to generate the sample data, then review the values before relying on them
Use Copilot to generate the data and treat it as validated production data
Ask Copilot to translate the form to another language instead
Avoid Copilot because it cannot create test data

questions.explanation: Under "automating the boring stuff" and the SDLC Testing & QA phase, Copilot quickly creates realistic sample/test data. However, generated data is illustrative and must be reviewed (types, edge values, sensitive-looking values) before use.
questions.domain_title: Improve developer productivity with GitHub Copilot

questions.stem: A developer must modify an undocumented legacy module they've never seen. During the SDLC Maintenance & support phase, how can Copilot help them get started?

options.text: By automatically deploying the module to production
By providing explanations of the code and suggesting modern equivalents
By deleting the legacy code and rewriting the architecture on its own
By enforcing a required review policy on the pull request

questions.explanation: In maintenance, Copilot helps developers understand unfamiliar or legacy code by providing explanations and modern equivalents, and can suggest refactoring and cross-language translation to modernize it. It does not autonomously redesign or deploy the system.
questions.domain_title: Improve developer productivity with GitHub Copilot

questions.stem: In VS Code, a developer wants Copilot to generate xUnit tests for the file open in the editor and then run them, confirming each tool call as it happens. Which Chat view configuration and command best fits?

options.text: Ask mode with Autopilot, using /explain
Agent mode with Default Approvals, using /tests (or a prompt that asks it to run the tests)
Plan mode with Bypass Approvals, using /setupTests
Ask mode with Default Approvals, using /fixTestFailure

questions.explanation: Agent mode can write and run tests, and /tests generates tests for the active code by detecting the existing framework and style. Default Approvals keeps the developer confirming each tool call. Ask is read-only; /setupTests configures a framework rather than generating tests.
questions.domain_title: Improve developer productivity with GitHub Copilot

questions.stem: A team wants every generated test to use xUnit and follow Arrange-Act-Assert, and they need integration tests for a data access layer. Which statements are correct about how Copilot supports these goals in VS Code? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: A *.instructions.md file with an applyTo field (e.g., applyTo: tests/**) can enforce framework and naming conventions across the team
Integration tests are produced from a natural-language prompt, such as "Create integration tests for the data access layer in this module"
A dedicated /integrationTests slash command is required to create integration tests
Custom instructions can be shared in source control so every developer gets the same conventions

questions.explanation: Custom instructions in a *.instructions.md file (with applyTo scoping) enforce framework, naming, and Arrange-Act-Assert conventions and can be shared in source control. Integration tests come from natural-language prompts, not a dedicated slash command.
questions.domain_title: Improve developer productivity with GitHub Copilot

questions.stem: Before writing any tests, a developer wants Copilot to list the edge cases for a CalculateDiscount method and explain why each one matters — without modifying any files. Which Chat mode should they use?

options.text: Agent mode, so it writes and runs the tests immediately
Ask mode, attaching the code with #selection to brainstorm edge cases
Autopilot permission level, so it applies changes without prompting
Plan mode with Bypass Approvals to edit the test file directly

questions.explanation: Ask mode answers questions and analyzes code without modifying files or invoking tools — ideal for exploring edge cases first. You can attach the code with #selection, then switch to Agent to generate the tests once the scenarios are clear.
questions.domain_title: Improve developer productivity with GitHub Copilot

questions.stem: A test class already contains two Arrange-Act-Assert tests, and the method under test is imported. What is the fastest way to add one more specific edge-case test using ghost text?

options.text: Place the cursor after the last test, type a descriptive comment for the case, and accept the inline completion with Tab
Run /setupTests to reconfigure the framework before Copilot can suggest anything
Open a new empty file with no existing patterns and start typing
Use the Fix Test Failure sparkle button to add the new case

questions.explanation: Ghost text works best when the file already shows the pattern (Arrange-Act-Assert) and the method is imported. Typing a descriptive comment for the new edge case steers the inline completion; Tab accepts and Esc dismisses. A clear comment yields better assertions than a bare stub.
questions.domain_title: Improve developer productivity with GitHub Copilot

questions.stem: True or false: Because Copilot can suggest security fixes, its generated code is safe to merge without a human security review.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. Generated code may not follow security best practices and can contain bugs, misread context, and reflect training-data bias. Security- and performance-sensitive suggestions are drafts to validate — careful human review is non-negotiable.
questions.domain_title: Improve developer productivity with GitHub Copilot

questions.stem: A single unit test is failing and the developer wants a fast, focused fix triggered from within Test Explorer, with the failing test and its output attached automatically. Which feature should they use?

options.text: The Fix Test Failure (sparkle) button in Test Explorer
The /setupTests command in Chat
The Add Context button to attach #codebase
Ghost text after the failing test

questions.explanation: Test Explorer's Fix Test Failure (sparkle) button attaches the failing test and its output and proposes a targeted fix — to the test, the app code, or both. The equivalent /fixTestFailure works from Chat, and an Agent can monitor output and auto-fix until green.
questions.domain_title: Improve developer productivity with GitHub Copilot

questions.stem: A repository administrator at a company on GitHub Copilot Business wants to stop a folder of proprietary configuration files from ever informing Copilot code-completion suggestions. Where must this be configured?

options.text: Each developer enables a per-file exclusion from the Copilot menu in their IDE.
In the repository's Settings under Copilot, in 'Repositories and paths to exclude'.
In a .copilotignore file committed to the root of the repository.
In the personal account settings of the repository owner under Copilot > Features.

questions.explanation: Content exclusions are configured only in organization or repository settings. At the repository level the path is Settings > Copilot > 'Repositories and paths to exclude'. There is no editor-side field for exclusions, no .copilotignore mechanism, and personal-account settings do not define content exclusions.
questions.domain_title: Configure privacy, content exclusions, and safeguards

questions.stem: After an administrator excludes a file with a content exclusion, what does Copilot stop doing with that file? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: It stops offering code completion inside the excluded file.
It stops using the file's content to inform code-completion suggestions in other files.
It stops using the file's content to inform Copilot Chat responses.
It deletes the excluded file from the repository.

questions.explanation: Excluding content has three effects: code completion is disabled in the affected file, that file no longer informs completions in other files, and it no longer informs Copilot Chat responses. Exclusion only limits how Copilot uses the file as context; it never deletes or modifies the file.
questions.domain_title: Configure privacy, content exclusions, and safeguards

questions.stem: A team lead on a Copilot Pro plan asks to exclude a /secrets directory from Copilot. What should you tell them?

options.text: They can add the exclusion in their editor settings on any plan.
Content exclusions require Copilot Business or Enterprise and are set in org/repository settings, so Pro cannot do this.
They should switch the public-code filter to Block, which excludes the folder.
They can exclude it by adding the folder to their personal Copilot Privacy settings.

questions.explanation: Content exclusions are a Business/Enterprise capability configured in organization or repository settings; Free, Pro, and Pro+ have no content-exclusion feature. The public-code filter limits output matching public code and is unrelated to excluding a folder from context.
questions.domain_title: Configure privacy, content exclusions, and safeguards

questions.stem: An enterprise on GitHub Copilot Enterprise wants GitHub to assume legal responsibility if a Copilot suggestion is challenged as infringing third-party IP. Besides being on Business/Enterprise, what must be true?

options.text: The 'Suggestions matching public code' setting must be set to Block.
Every developer must sign a Data Protection Agreement individually.
Content exclusions must be configured for all repositories.
The 'Suggestions matching public code' setting must be set to Allow.

questions.explanation: IP indemnity is included with Business and Enterprise, but GitHub only assumes that responsibility when the Matching public code setting is set to Block. Allow does not activate indemnity, and neither the DPA signing nor content exclusions are the condition for indemnity.
questions.domain_title: Configure privacy, content exclusions, and safeguards

questions.stem: True or False: Because Copilot Business includes IP indemnity, developers no longer need to review, test, or validate the suggestions they accept.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. Copilot is a generative-AI assistant whose output can be wrong or biased; the developer owns and is responsible for accepted code. Contractual protections like IP indemnity add legal coverage but do not remove the need for human oversight, review, and validation.
questions.domain_title: Configure privacy, content exclusions, and safeguards

questions.stem: Roughly what kind of suggestion does the 'suggestions matching public code' duplication filter act on when set to Block?

options.text: Any suggestion containing a URL to a public repository.
Suggestions of roughly 150 or more characters that closely match publicly available code on GitHub.
Every suggestion, regardless of length, that references an open-source license.
Short one-line snippets under 20 characters that appear in many repositories.

questions.explanation: The duplication-detection filter targets longer look-alikes — suggestions of about 150+ characters that closely match public code — and blocks or truncates them. Short snippets are not the target, and the filter is about code matches, not URLs or license references.
questions.domain_title: Configure privacy, content exclusions, and safeguards

questions.stem: A developer on a Pro plan whose seat is provided by their organization finds the 'Suggestions matching public code' toggle greyed out and cannot change it. Why?

options.text: The filter is only available on Business and Enterprise plans.
For an organization-provided seat the toggle may be locked and reflects the organization's policy.
The toggle is disabled until the developer enables content exclusions.
The setting was moved and no longer exists for personal accounts.

questions.explanation: Individually paid personal users fully control the Allow/Block toggle under Copilot > Features > Privacy, but for an organization-provided seat the toggle may be locked to the org's policy. The public-code filter is available on all plans, so plan availability is not the cause.
questions.domain_title: Configure privacy, content exclusions, and safeguards

questions.stem: Which statements correctly distinguish content exclusions from the suggestions-matching-public-code filter? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Content exclusions limit which files Copilot uses as context; the public-code filter limits output that matches public code.
Content exclusions are Business/Enterprise only, while the public-code filter is available on all plans.
Both features are configured per file inside the editor by individual developers.
Setting the public-code filter to Block is what activates IP indemnity at Business/Enterprise.

questions.explanation: Content exclusions restrict the context Copilot draws from (Business/Enterprise only, set in org/repo settings), whereas the public-code filter restricts output that matches public code and is available on all plans, with Block enabling IP indemnity. Neither is configured per file inside the editor, so option C is false.
questions.domain_title: Configure privacy, content exclusions, and safeguards

questions.stem: An admin adds a content exclusion, but a developer who already had the IDE open still receives suggestions in the excluded file. What is the most likely cause and fix?

options.text: The exclusion failed to save; re-enter it in personal Copilot settings.
Propagation delay — changes can take up to 30 minutes in an already-loaded IDE; reload the content-exclusion settings to apply immediately.
The developer must set the public-code filter to Block for exclusions to work.
Content exclusions never apply to files that are already open and require closing the repository.

questions.explanation: Exclusion changes can take up to 30 minutes to take effect in an IDE that already loaded the settings; reloading the content-exclusion settings applies them immediately. The public-code filter is unrelated, and exclusions are not defined in personal settings.
questions.domain_title: Configure privacy, content exclusions, and safeguards

questions.stem: A developer wants to quickly confirm whether the file they are editing is currently under a content exclusion. What is the fastest signal to check?

options.text: Run a full repository scan from the GitHub web UI.
The Copilot status-bar icon shows a diagonal line when an exclusion applies; hovering reveals whether an org or the parent repository disabled Copilot for that file.
Open the file's Git blame to see the exclusion author.
Check the developer's internet connection status.

questions.explanation: When an exclusion applies to the current file, the Copilot status-bar icon has a diagonal line through it, and hovering shows whether an org or the parent repository disabled Copilot for that file. Git blame, a repo scan, and connection status do not indicate per-file exclusion state.
questions.domain_title: Configure privacy, content exclusions, and safeguards
